original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded Loop in CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() Causes Permanent Fund Lock
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: distribute() fetches ALL curator addresses via getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) and iterates through them twice (once for share counting, once for reward calculation). For popular creds with many curators, this exceeds the block gas limit, permanently locking accumulated rewards.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function has two unbounded loops over all curators plus an external call to getShareNumber per curator. Each curator adds ~20K+ gas. At ~30M gas limit, approximately 1000-1500 curators would make distribution impossible. MAX_SUPPLY is 999, meaning up to 999 unique curators could exist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two loops over distributeAddresses (unbounded array from getCuratorAddresses(credId,0,0)) with external call getShareNumber per iteration

preconditions: A cred accumulates many unique curators (up to 999 from MAX_SUPPLY)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) fetches ALL addresses; two full iteration loops with external calls
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No pagination support in distribute(); balanceOf[credId] can never be zeroed if gas exceeds limit
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum curator limit, no pagination, no batch distribution mechanism

analysis_questions: At what curator count does distribute() exceed 30M gas?
Can an attacker deliberately inflate curator count by buying 1 share from many addresses?
Is there a fallback mechanism to recover locked rewards?
validation_requirements: Simulate distribute() with 500, 750, 999 curators and measure gas
Verify no alternative withdrawal path exists for locked balanceOf[credId]

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker creates many wallets and buys 1 share each for a target credId (up to 999 unique addresses). 2. Rewards accumulate in CuratorRewardsDistributor for this credId. 3. distribute(credId) exceeds gas limit. 4. ETH is permanently locked in the contract.
prerequisites: ETH to buy 1 share from many addresses (bonding curve price increases with supply)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All accumulated curator rewards for affected credId are permanently locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All curators of the affected credId
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery mechanism exists; contract is not upgradeable

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No gas limit test for distribute() with many curators

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Critical - need exact gas per curator to determine threshold
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sum of bonding curve prices for N shares
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Setup requires multiple transactions; DoS is permanent

validation_result.impact_summary: distribute() iterates over all curator addresses (potentially 999+ unique entries that only grow) with external calls per iteration. When gas exceeds block limit, accumulated rewards in balanceOf[credId] are permanently locked with no recovery path.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() function fetches ALL curator addresses via getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) and iterates through them twice with external calls. Additionally, the underlying EnumerableMap never removes entries (set with value 0 retains the key), meaning the iteration count grows monotonically with every unique curator who ever held shares, not just current holders. The function then calls depositBatch which also loops over all addresses. With MAX_SUPPLY=999, up to 999 concurrent holders can exist, but the map can grow even larger from past holders. The CuratorRewardsDistributor is non-upgradeable (uses constructor-based Ownable2Step, not UUPS) and has no alternative withdrawal mechanism for balanceOf[credId]. distribute() is the ONLY code path that reduces balanceOf[credId].
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof-of-signal at CuratorRewardsDistributor.sol lines 77-118 accurately describes the vulnerability. Two unbounded loops with external calls (getShareNumber) plus the initial getCuratorAddresses call that itself iterates all map entries. EnumerableMap entries are never removed via shareBalance[credId_].set(sender_, 0) in _updateCuratorShareBalance, causing monotonic growth of the iteration space.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Two unbounded loops over all curator addresses with external calls (getShareNumber) per iteration. getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) fetches ALL entries. No pagination support. With hundreds of curators, gas exceeds block limit, permanently locking balanceOf[credId].

On sell, shareBalance[credId_].set(sender_, 0) does NOT remove the key from the EnumerableMap - it just sets value to 0. The entry persists, causing shareBalance[credId_].length() to grow monotonically. This means the iteration space in distribute() only increases over time.

When stop_=0, iterates ALL entries in EnumerableMap including zero-balance entries that were never removed. Each .at() call involves storage reads. This contributes significant gas to the overall getCuratorAddresses call.

calling_sequence: 2. credContract.getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) → _getCuratorData iterates ALL EnumerableMap entries with storage reads
3. First loop: credContract.getShareNumber(credId, addr) called for EACH curator (external call + storage read)
4. Second loop: credContract.getShareNumber(credId, addr) called AGAIN for EACH curator
5. phiRewardsContract.depositBatch() called with all addresses and amounts (third loop with storage writes)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: distribute() → getCuratorAddresses(credId, 0, 0) iterates shareBalance[credId_].length() entries → loop 1 makes N external calls to getShareNumber → loop 2 makes N more external calls to getShareNumber → depositBatch loops through N recipients with storage writes. Total: ~4N external calls + N storage writes + 2N storage reads in getCuratorAddresses. With N=999 (MAX_SUPPLY) or higher (due to non-removal of zero-balance entries), gas exceeds practical limits. balanceOf[credId] can only be zeroed through distribute(), and CuratorRewardsDistributor is non-upgradeable.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deploy.s.sol shows: curatorRewardsDistributor = new CuratorRewardsDistributor(address(phiRewards), payable(address(credProxy))); - deployed with constructor, no proxy pattern. Contract inherits Ownable2Step (not UUPSUpgradeable).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is straightforward: (1) Attacker creates many wallets and buys 1 share each for a target credId. Cost increases along the bonding curve but is bounded. (2) Rewards accumulate in CuratorRewardsDistributor via PhiRewards.depositRewards calling curatorRewardsDistributor.deposit. (3) When enough curators exist, distribute(credId) exceeds block gas limit. (4) ETH in balanceOf[credId] is permanently locked. Even without an attacker, organic growth of a popular cred could naturally reach hundreds of unique curators. Furthermore, since the EnumerableMap never removes zero-balance entries, even curators who sell all shares continue to inflate the iteration count.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. All accumulated curator rewards for any credId with sufficiently many (historical) curators become permanently irrecoverable. The CuratorRewardsDistributor is not upgradeable, and distribute() is the only function that can reduce balanceOf[credId]. There is no emergency withdrawal mechanism. The owner can only update phiRewardsContract and withdrawRoyalty - neither provides access to locked funds.
recommended_mitigations: Implement paginated/batched distribution: allow distribute() to process a subset of curators at a time, tracking progress across multiple calls
Add a pull-based reward claiming mechanism where individual curators can claim their proportional share
Remove zero-balance entries from the EnumerableMap when curators sell all shares (use .remove() instead of .set(addr, 0))
Add an emergency withdrawal function gated by owner/timelock for stuck funds
Consider making CuratorRewardsDistributor upgradeable to allow future fixes
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Cred and CuratorRewardsDistributor on a test chain. 2. Create a cred. 3. Use a loop to buy 1 share from ~500-999 unique addresses. 4. Deposit rewards via PhiRewards flow. 5. Call distribute(credId) and measure gas. 6. Increase curator count and verify gas exceeds 30M limit. 7. Confirm no alternative withdrawal path exists.
uncertainties: Exact gas threshold depends on chain-specific gas limits (L2 vs L1) and warm/cold storage access patterns
The actual number of curators needed to trigger DoS depends on specific gas costs of EnumerableMap operations
On some L2s with higher effective gas limits, the threshold may be higher than 999
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of distribute() with 100, 250, 500, 750, 999 curators to find exact threshold
Test on target L2 chain (Base, per deployment scripts) with actual gas limits
Verify that EnumerableMap entries persist after selling to 0 balance (confirming monotonic growth)
Calculate economic cost of inflating curator count via bonding curve prices
